one_line_takeaway: A 300-task, trajectory-audited agent benchmark showing that embedded-safety + evidence-grounded grading catches ~44% more safety violations than an LLM judge, and that agent reliability (Pass^k) collapses under transient failures even when peak capability (Pass@k) looks stable.
key_finding: Trajectory-opaque evaluation is systematically unreliable: a vanilla LLM judge (given the full transcript and grader source) misses 12/27 (44%) of safety violations and 15/118 (13%) of robustness issues that the hybrid pipeline catches, because deterministic tool-parameter matching catches violations the LLM rationalizes away. Capability does not imply consistency: under error injection Pass@3 stays roughly flat while Pass^3 drops by up to 24 percentage points and the Pass@3-Pass^3 gap widens monotonically for every model. Agent capability is strongly multi-dimensional: rankings shift across task groups (Claude-Opus-4.6 leads General Pass^3 at 70.4% but is 2nd in Multimodal; GPT-5.4 is 3rd in General but 1st in Multimodal), and no model dominates all multimodal domains. In multi-turn dialogue, question precision explains 76% of Pass^3 variance (r=0.87) whereas round count explains under 1% (r=0.07) - better questions, not more of them.
core_contribution: Claw-Eval is an end-to-end evaluation suite for LLM autonomous agents built by the authors (Peking University / HKU) to close three gaps they identify in prior agent benchmarks: (G1) trajectory-opaque grading, (G2) underspecified safety and robustness, and (G3) narrow task coverage. Its own three-part contribution is: (1) an auditable execution pipeline that separates each run into Setup, Execution, and Judge phases behind a 'temporal firewall' and records three independent evidence channels (structured execution traces, service-side audit logs, post-execution environment snapshots) so grading verifies what the agent actually did rather than self-report; (2) a cross-modal suite of 300 human-verified tasks over 9 categories (General 161, Multimodal 101, Multi-turn Dialogue 38) decomposed into 2,159 fine-grained rubric items (avg 7.2/task), with safety constraints embedded directly inside ordinary workflow tasks and robustness measured via controlled tool-call error injection; and (3) an integrated multi-dimensional scoring protocol over Completion, Safety, and Robustness reported as Average Score, Pass@k, and Pass^k across trials. NOTE: benchmarks the authors merely cite as prior/related work (SWE-bench, tau-bench, ToolEmu, R-Judge, Agent-SafetyBench, MobileRisk-Live, Terminal-Bench, Toolathlon, OSWorld, WebArena, etc.) are NOT Claw-Eval's contribution; the paper's claim is that none of them simultaneously supports multimodal + multi-turn + auditable + embedded-safety + perturbation + sandboxed evaluation.
threat_model: This is an evaluation/measurement paper, not an attack paper, so there is no adversarial attacker in the classic sense. The security-relevant risk model is agent deployment safety: (1) agents may violate embedded policy/safety constraints during multi-step tool-use workflows (e.g. forbidden actions detected via deterministic matching on tool-call parameters); (2) agents may reward-hack or fabricate intermediate steps that output-only / trajectory-opaque grading cannot catch, so a benign-looking transcript can hide a real violation; and (3) agents may be non-robust to transient infrastructure failures (rate limits, server errors, latency) and fail to recover in deployment. The paper's central safety finding is that a vanilla LLM-as-judge, even given the full transcript and grader source, misses a large fraction of the violations that the hybrid deterministic+LLM pipeline catches, so trajectory-opaque evaluation systematically under-reports safety risk. The threat model is deployment-risk / evaluation-integrity, not a specific exploit against a target system.
limitations: Stated by the authors in Appendix A: (1) Perturbation coverage is limited to infrastructure-level transient failures (HTTP 429/500, latency spikes); semantic-level and adversarial perturbations are left to future work, so the robustness axis does not cover prompt-injection-style or content-based attacks. (2) Judgment calibration: open-ended rubric items depend on LLM judges, which reach 95% exact agreement with human scoring but remain an approximation that may not generalize to future task types. (3) Benchmark longevity: like any public benchmark it can be gamed/optimized against, though trajectory-evidence channels and deterministic safety gates are offered as anti-shortcut mechanisms. Reviewer-observed additional caveats: Multi-turn is only 38 tasks (small per-domain n, e.g. STEM=10) yet drives headline correlation claims; Multimodal is restricted to 9 vision-capable models; and using Claude-Opus-4.6 as both simulated user and judge in Multi-turn, plus Gemini-3-Flash as judge while it is also an evaluated model, introduces potential self-preference confounds the paper does not fully control.

datasets_used: Claw-Eval (the authors' own benchmark): 300 human-verified tasks across 9 categories in 3 groups
Multimodal group: 101 tasks (Video 53, Doc & Image 22, Code 26)
Multi-turn Dialogue group: 38 tasks (STEM 10, Social Science 13, Business 15)
2,159 fine-grained rubric items total, averaging 7.2 per task
quantitative_results: Vanilla LLM judge misses 12/27 (44%) of safety violations detected by the hybrid pipeline
Vanilla LLM judge misses 15/118 (13%) of robustness issues detected by the hybrid pipeline
Overall best Pass^3 = 70.4% (Claude Opus 4.6); best average Score = 81.4% (Claude Sonnet 4.6)
GPT 5.4 overall: Score 78.4, Pass@3 78.4, Pass^3 60.3; Gemini 3.1 Pro overall Pass^3 57.8; worst Nemotron 3 Super overall Pass^3 5.5

Multimodal is much harder: highest Pass^3 only 25.7% (GPT 5.4) vs 70.8% Opus-4.6 on General; multimodal per-domain leaders differ (Video: Opus/Sonnet 15.4%, Doc & Image: GPT-5.4 54.5%, Code: MiMo-V2-Omni 33.3%)
Under error injection Pass^3 drops by up to 24 percentage points while Pass@3 stays roughly flat; at rate 0.6 Claude-Opus-4.6 retains 56.5% Pass^3 with gap widening 9.9% -> 20.5%
Error-injection composition: HTTP 429 (35%), HTTP 500 (35%), 2-4s latency spike (30%)
Multi-turn: question precision r=0.87 (R^2=0.76, 76% of Pass^3 variance) vs round count r=0.07 (R^2<0.01, under 1%)
General Pass^3 by difficulty (Claude Opus 4.6): Easy 74.7, Medium 70.2, Hard 65.1; all models degrade monotonically Easy->Hard (Easy spans 14%-75% across 6 models)
Multimodal consistency ratio rho = Pass^3/Pass@3: Video 0.37, Code 0.48, Doc & Image 0.53; Video Pass^3 averages only 10.7% vs Doc & Image 32.3%, Code 23.9%
LLM judges reach 95% exact agreement with human scoring (Appendix F.3); median trial-to-trial sigma = 1.2% on General
baselines_compared: Methodological baseline: vanilla single-call LLM-as-judge (Gemini-3-Flash, given full transcript + grader source, audit logs and snapshots withheld) vs the authors' hybrid deterministic+LLM grading pipeline

14 evaluated models across 10 families: Claude-Opus-4.6, Claude-Sonnet-4.6, GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Gemini-3-Flash, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, MiMo-V2-Pro, MiMo-V2-Omni, GLM-5-Turbo, GLM-5V-Turbo, DeepSeek-V3.2, MiniMax-M2.7, Kimi-K2.5, Nemotron-3-Super (Multimodal restricted to the 9 vision-capable models)
Prior benchmarks contrasted in Table 1 (cited, not Claw-Eval's own results): AgentBench, GAIA, tau-bench, SWE-bench, WebArena, VisualWebArena, OSWorld, ToolBench, Terminal-Bench, PinchBench
Metric baselines: Average Score, Pass@3 (any of 3 trials exceeds 0.75), Pass^3 (all 3 trials pass) to separate capability ceiling from reliability floor
